topics.title: Class & Object in Java
topics.description: A class is a blueprint — it defines fields (state) and methods (behavior). An object is an instance of a class — a concrete allocation on the heap with its own copy of instance fields. Java's entire type system is built around classes and objects. Understanding the distinction between the blueprint and its instances is the foundation of Object-Oriented Programming.
topics.story: Think of a class as a cookie cutter and objects as cookies. The cutter (class) defines the shape — it has a template for what fields (chocolate chips, size, flavor) and behaviors (bake(), eat()) exist. Each cookie (object) is a separate, independent instance with its own values — one can be chocolate chip, another oatmeal raisin. The cutter never changes regardless of how many cookies you make. When you call new Cookie(), you press the cutter into dough and get a new cookie on the heap.
topics.analogy: Class = architectural blueprint for a house. Object = the actual house built from it. You can build 1000 houses from one blueprint — each with its own address, paint color, and furniture (field values). The blueprint itself doesn't have a paint color — instances do. Similarly, a Java class definition has no data of its own (except static fields); instances do.
topics.memoryAnchor: COOKIE CUTTER mnemonic — Class = the cutter (template, loaded ONCE into Method Area). Object = the cookie (instance, created on Heap per new). One cutter makes unlimited cookies. Constructor = decorating a fresh cookie immediately after cutting. 'this' = the cookie currently in your hand. 'static' = belongs to the cutter (class), not any individual cookie. Key locations: class metadata lives in Method Area; field values live in Heap; references live in Stack. new ClassName() = allocate heap space + run constructor + return reference.
topics.firstPrinciples: Before OOP, programs were procedures acting on global data — hard to reason about, easy to corrupt. OOP bundles data and the methods that operate on it into objects. Encapsulation means outside code can't corrupt the object's state directly (through access modifiers). Objects communicate by calling each other's methods — the object decides how to respond, maintaining its own invariants.
topics.bruteForce: Without classes: represent a bank account as parallel arrays — balances[], accountIds[], owners[]. To transfer money between accounts: manually update two array slots. Any function can corrupt any account. Error-prone, hard to maintain, impossible to ensure invariants (balance never goes negative).
topics.optimizedApproach: With classes: BankAccount encapsulates balance privately. deposit() and withdraw() enforce invariants (no negative balance). Objects own their data and expose safe operations. Multiple BankAccount instances can coexist independently. Code that uses BankAccount doesn't know how balance is stored internally.
topics.whenToUse: Classes are used for everything in Java — even the entry point is a class. Design a class when: you have data and related operations that belong together, you need multiple independent instances, you want to enforce invariants through encapsulation, or you want to model a real-world entity (User, Order, Product). Static utility methods (Math, Collections) are the exception — stateless, no instances needed.

examples.title: Class Definition and Object Creation
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":33,"lineCode":"BankAccount alice = new BankAccount(\"Alice\", 1000.0);","variables":{"alice":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"new allocates BankAccount on heap at 0x100. Constructor runs: owner='Alice', balance=1000.0"},{"line":34,"lineCode":"BankAccount bob = new BankAccount(\"Bob\", 500.0);","variables":{"alice":"ref@0x100","bob":"ref@0x200"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Second independent object at 0x200. alice and bob have completely separate field copies on the heap"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"alice.deposit(250.0);","variables":{"alice.balance":1250.0,"bob.balance":500.0},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"deposit() called on alice's object only. bob's balance is untouched"},{"line":37,"lineCode":"boolean ok = bob.withdraw(600.0);","variables":{"ok":false,"bob.balance":500.0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"600 > 500 — invariant enforced. Returns false, bob's balance unchanged"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph BankAccount_Blueprint
      F[fields: owner, balance, accountId]
      M[methods: deposit, withdraw, getBalance]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      A[alice@0x100: owner=Alice balance=1250]
      B[bob@0x200: owner=Bob balance=500]
    end
    BankAccount_Blueprint -->|new → instance 1| A
    BankAccount_Blueprint -->|new → instance 2| B
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS BankAccount:
  blueprint: fields + methods (loaded once, Method Area)

NEW BankAccount(owner, balance):
  1. allocate heap space for fields
  2. run constructor to initialize
  3. return reference

Each instance: own field values on heap
All instances: share same method bytecode
examples.explanation: The class definition (blueprint) is loaded once into the Method Area. Each new BankAccount(...) allocates a separate object on the heap with its own copy of owner, balance, and accountId. Methods are shared — the same bytecode serves all instances — but 'this' inside each method refers to the specific object it was called on. alice.deposit() cannot affect bob's balance.
examples.realWorldUse: Every Java framework object is an instance: Spring Beans (@Service creates one instance per scope), JPA Entities (each DB row becomes one object), HTTP request objects (each request gets a fresh HttpServletRequest instance). The class defines the shape; the framework or your code creates instances at runtime.

examples.title: this Keyword — Three Uses
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"this.width = width;","variables":{"this.width":4.0,"width_param":4.0},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"this.width = field on the heap object. width (no this) = the constructor parameter. Same name — this. resolves ambiguity"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"this(side, side, \"Square\");","variables":{"side":5.0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"this() delegates to the 3-arg constructor. Must be first statement. Avoids duplicating initialization logic"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"this.width *= factor;","variables":{"this.width":8.0,"factor":2.0},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Modifies the field of THIS specific Rectangle object"},{"line":22,"lineCode":"return this;","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Returns reference to current object — caller can chain another method call on the same object"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph this_three_uses
      A[this.field — distinguish field from param]
      B[this(...) — chain to another constructor]
      C[return this — enable method chaining]
    end
    r[Rectangle r] -->|r.scale(2.0)| s[scale: this=r, returns r]
    s -->|.relabel('Doubled')| l[relabel: this=r, returns r]
examples.pseudocode:
this = hidden reference to current object instance

USE 1: this.field vs param of same name
USE 2: this(args) → delegate to another ctor (first line only)
USE 3: return this → fluent chaining API
examples.explanation: Three distinct uses of 'this': (1) Disambiguate field name from parameter — this.width refers to the heap field, width alone refers to the constructor parameter. (2) Constructor chaining this(...) — must be the first statement, delegates to another constructor of the same class to avoid duplication. (3) Return this — enables fluent APIs where method calls chain together on the same object.
examples.realWorldUse: Return this powers builder patterns everywhere: StringBuilder.append().append().toString(), AssertJ assertions assertThat(x).isNotNull().isGreaterThan(0), JPA criteria queries, Spring MockMvcRequestBuilders. Lombok @Builder generates a builder class where every setter returns this.

examples.title: Static vs Instance Members
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":25,"lineCode":"System.out.println(Counter.getTotal());","variables":{"totalCreated":0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Static method called on the CLASS itself. No instance needed. totalCreated=0 from Method Area"},{"line":28,"lineCode":"Counter a = new Counter(\"A\");","variables":{"a":"ref@0x100","totalCreated":1},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Constructor runs: a.name='A', a.count=0 on heap. totalCreated++ → 1 in Method Area (shared!)"},{"line":29,"lineCode":"Counter b = new Counter(\"B\");","variables":{"b":"ref@0x200","totalCreated":2},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"b at 0x200. totalCreated → 2. Both objects share this static value"},{"line":31,"lineCode":"a.increment(); a.increment();","variables":{"a.count":2,"b.count":0,"totalCreated":2},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Only a.count changes. b.count stays 0. totalCreated unchanged — increment() doesn't touch it"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Method_Area_one_copy
      S1[static totalCreated = 2]
      S2[static VERSION = '1.0']
    end
    subgraph Heap_per_instance
      A[a@0x100: count=2, name='A']
      B[b@0x200: count=1, name='B']
    end
    S1 -.->|shared by all| A
    S1 -.->|shared by all| B
examples.pseudocode:
STATIC field/method:
  one copy in Method Area
  accessed: ClassName.member (or instance.member — but misleading)
  no implicit 'this' — cannot access instance fields directly

INSTANCE field/method:
  one copy per object on heap
  accessed: object.member or this.member inside class
  independent per object
examples.explanation: Static members belong to the class, not any object. totalCreated is ONE variable in the Method Area shared by every Counter — any constructor incrementing it affects all. Instance fields (count, name) have separate copies per object — a.count=2 never affects b.count=1. Static methods have no 'this' — they can't reference instance fields directly.
examples.realWorldUse: Common static usage: constants (Math.PI, Integer.MAX_VALUE), factory methods (Integer.valueOf()), utility methods (Collections.sort()), singletons (getInstance()). Anti-pattern: mutable static state in multi-threaded programs causes race conditions — use AtomicInteger or synchronization.

examples.title: equals() and hashCode() Contract
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":32,"lineCode":"System.out.println(p1 == p2);","variables":{"p1":"ref@0x100","p2":"ref@0x200"},"phase":"COMPARE","annotation":"== compares heap addresses: 0x100 ≠ 0x200 → false. Two distinct objects"},{"line":33,"lineCode":"System.out.println(p1.equals(p2));","variables":{"p1.id":"P001","p2.id":"P001"},"phase":"COMPARE","annotation":"equals() delegates to id.equals(p.id): 'P001'.equals('P001') → true. Logical equality"},{"line":38,"lineCode":"stock.put(p1, 100);","variables":{"bucket":"hash('P001')"},"phase":"PUT","annotation":"HashMap: compute p1.hashCode() → pick bucket → store entry (key=p1, value=100)"},{"line":39,"lineCode":"stock.get(p2)","variables":{"bucket":"hash('P001')"},"phase":"GET","annotation":"p2.hashCode() = hash('P001') = same bucket. p2.equals(p1) = true → entry found → returns 100"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[HashMap.get key] --> B[key.hashCode → bucket]
    B --> C[scan bucket entries]
    C --> D{key.equals entry?}
    D -->|true| E[return value]
    D -->|false| F[next entry / null]
    G[Contract] --> H[equals true → hashCode MUST match]
    G --> I[hashCode match → equals MAY match]
examples.pseudocode:
DEFAULT Object.equals() → == reference comparison
DEFAULT Object.hashCode() → identity hash (address-based)

OVERRIDE BOTH or neither:
  equals: same logical content → true
  hashCode: consistent with equals (same fields)

BREAKING CONTRACT:
  put(p1, v) then get(p2) where p1.equals(p2) → null!
  (different bucket found, entry never located)
examples.explanation: Default equals() from Object compares references. Overriding it to compare by ID means two separate objects with the same ID are logically equal. hashCode must be consistent — same fields as equals. Violating this breaks HashMap/HashSet: put(p1) stores in bucket hash(p1); get(p2) looks in bucket hash(p2); if they differ, the entry is never found.
examples.realWorldUse: JPA entities override equals/hashCode on primary key — critical for Set/Map behavior in Hibernate sessions. Records (Java 14+) auto-generate both based on all fields. Lombok @EqualsAndHashCode generates them. IntelliJ IDEA's 'Generate equals() and hashCode()' wizard follows the Objects.hash() pattern.

examples.title: Object Lifecycle — Creation, Use, GC
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":25,"lineCode":"Resource r = new Resource(\"Manual\");","variables":{"r":"ref@0x100","open":true},"phase":"CREATE","annotation":"Heap object created. Constructor prints 'CREATED'. Phase 1: birth"},{"line":26,"lineCode":"r.use();","variables":{"open":true},"phase":"USE","annotation":"Phase 2: in-use. Calling use() after close() would throw IllegalStateException"},{"line":27,"lineCode":"r.close();","variables":{"open":false},"phase":"CLOSE","annotation":"Phase 3: explicitly closed. open=false prevents further use"},{"line":28,"lineCode":"r = null;","variables":{"r":null},"phase":"RELEASE","annotation":"Last reference removed. Object at 0x100 is now GC-eligible"},{"line":31,"lineCode":"try (Resource r2 = new Resource(\"AutoClose\")) {","variables":{"r2":"ref@0x200"},"phase":"CREATE","annotation":"try-with-resources: r2 created inside try header"},{"line":33,"lineCode":"} // implicit r2.close()","variables":{},"phase":"AUTO_CLOSE","annotation":"JVM compiles to finally block: r2.close() ALWAYS called when try block exits"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[new Resource] -->|constructor| B[CREATED]
    B -->|.use()| C[IN USE]
    C -->|.close()| D[CLOSED]
    D -->|null or out of scope| E[GC ELIGIBLE]
    E -->|GC runs| F[COLLECTED]
    G[try-with-resources] -->|block exit| H[close auto-called]
    H --> E
examples.pseudocode:
LIFECYCLE:
  new X() → constructor → heap object (CREATED)
  x.method() → in use (USING)
  x.close() → explicit cleanup (CLOSED)
  x = null → GC eligible
  GC → memory freed (COLLECTED)

BEST PRACTICE:
  implement AutoCloseable
  use try-with-resources — guarantees close() even on exception
examples.explanation: Every Java object goes through: creation (constructor), use (method calls), cleanup (close()), GC eligibility (no strong references), and eventual collection. finalize() is deprecated since Java 9 — JVM may never call it, timing is undefined. For deterministic cleanup of I/O resources, implement AutoCloseable and always use try-with-resources.
examples.realWorldUse: FileInputStream, Connection, PreparedStatement all implement AutoCloseable. Always use: try (Connection c = ds.getConnection(); PreparedStatement ps = c.prepareStatement(sql)) { ... } — both closed in reverse order even if an exception is thrown. Forgetting to close causes file descriptor leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and socket leaks in production.

problems.title: Create a Student Class
problems.description:
Design a `Student` class with fields `String name`, `int age`, `double gpa`. Add a constructor, getters, and a `toString()` that returns `"Student{name='Alice', age=20, gpa=3.8}"`.

**Example 1:**
Student s = new Student("Alice", 20, 3.8);
s.toString() => "Student{name='Alice', age=20, gpa=3.8}"

**Example 2:**
Student s = new Student("Bob", 22, 3.2);
s.getName() => "Bob"
s.getAge() => 22
s.getGpa() => 3.2
problems.hint1: Use `this.name = name` in the constructor to initialize each field from its parameter.
problems.hint2: Getters: `public String getName() { return name; }` — one per field.
problems.hint3: toString(): return "Student{name=" + name + ", age=" + age + ", gpa=" + gpa + "}"
problems.editorial: Basic OOP building blocks: private fields (encapsulation), public constructor (initialization via this.x=x to disambiguate field from param), public getters (controlled access), @Override toString() for readable output. The pattern this.field = param is the canonical constructor body.

problems.title: Instance Counter With Static Field
problems.hint1: Declare `private static int nextId = 1;` — one shared copy in Method Area.
problems.hint2: In the constructor: `this.id = nextId++;` — post-increment assigns current then bumps.
problems.hint3: `getStaticCount()` can return `nextId - 1` (last assigned ID = total count).
problems.editorial: nextId is static — one value shared across all Widget instances. Each constructor assigns the current nextId to this.id then increments nextId (post-increment). getStaticCount() returns nextId-1 = last assigned ID = total count. In multi-threaded code use AtomicInteger.getAndIncrement() for thread safety.
problems.sampleInput: new Widget("button"), new Widget("label"), new Widget("panel")

problems.title: Override equals() and hashCode()
problems.description:
Create a `Point` class with `int x, y`. Override `equals()` so two Points with the same coordinates are equal, and override `hashCode()` consistently.

**Example 1:**
new Point(3,4).equals(new Point(3,4)) => true
new Point(3,4).equals(new Point(3,5)) => false

**Example 2:**
Set<Point> set = new HashSet<>();
set.add(new Point(1,2));
set.contains(new Point(1,2)) => true

**Key insight:** If you override `equals()`, you MUST override `hashCode()` — otherwise `HashSet`/`HashMap` will treat equal objects as different keys.
problems.hint1: equals(): check `if (!(o instanceof Point)) return false;` then cast and compare x and y.
problems.hint2: hashCode(): use `Objects.hash(x, y)` — uses the same two fields as equals.
problems.hint3: HashSet.add(p1); HashSet.add(p2) — if equals true, size stays 1 (deduplicated).
problems.editorial: Objects.hash(x, y) produces a hash from both fields. Both equals and hashCode MUST use the same fields — if equals uses x and y, hashCode must too. Breaking this breaks HashMap/HashSet. The instanceof check handles null and wrong type safely.

problems.title: Fluent Builder With Method Chaining
problems.description:
Implement a `PersonBuilder` that builds a `Person(name, age, email)` using **method chaining**. Each setter returns `this`, so calls can be chained. `build()` returns the final `Person`.

**Example:**
Person p = new PersonBuilder()
    .name("Alice")
    .age(30)
    .email("alice@example.com")
    .build();
p.getName() => "Alice"
p.getAge() => 30

**Key insight:** Each method returns `this` (the builder), enabling fluent dot-chaining. `build()` constructs the final immutable object.
problems.hint1: Each setter: `this.name = name; return this;`
problems.hint2: `build()` calls `new Person(name, age, email)` and returns it.
problems.hint3: Chain: `new PersonBuilder().name("Alice").age(30).email("a@b.com").build()`
problems.editorial: return this on every setter enables chaining — the dot chain keeps calling methods on the same PersonBuilder object. build() constructs the final immutable Person. This is the classic GoF Builder pattern, replicated in Lombok @Builder, Protobuf, OkHttp clients, and virtually every modern Java library.

problems.title: Descriptive toString()
problems.description:
Create a `Transaction` class with fields `String id`, `String type`, `double amount`, `java.time.LocalDate date`. Override `toString()` to return a human-readable summary.

**Example 1:**
new Transaction("T001", "CREDIT", 250.0, LocalDate.of(2024,1,15)).toString()
=> "Transaction[T001] CREDIT $250.00 on 2024-01-15"

**Example 2:**
new Transaction("T002", "DEBIT", 75.5, LocalDate.of(2024,2,1)).toString()
=> "Transaction[T002] DEBIT $75.50 on 2024-02-01"
problems.hint1: Use `String.format("$%.2f", amount)` to format the dollar amount with two decimals.
problems.hint2: Concatenate: `"Transaction[id=" + id + ", type=" + type + ", amount=" + formatted + ", at=" + timestamp + "]"`
problems.hint3: Add `@Override` to confirm you are overriding `Object.toString()` correctly.
problems.editorial: toString() is called automatically by System.out.println(obj), string concatenation ("val=" + obj), and logging frameworks. Without override, you get Transaction@6d06d69c — useless. String.format("%.2f", v) always produces exactly two decimal places.

problems.title: Multiple Constructors (Constructor Overloading)
problems.description:
Create a `Circle` class with **three constructors** using `this()` chaining:
- `Circle()` — defaults: radius=1.0, color="red"
- `Circle(double radius)` — default color="red"
- `Circle(double radius, String color)`

**Example 1:**
new Circle().getRadius() => 1.0, getColor() => "red"

**Example 2:**
new Circle(5.0).getRadius() => 5.0, getColor() => "red"

**Example 3:**
new Circle(3.0, "blue").getArea() => 28.27

**Key insight:** `this(args)` calls another constructor in the same class — avoids duplicating initialization logic.
problems.hint1: `Circle() { this(1.0, "red"); }` — chains to the 2-arg constructor.
problems.hint2: `Circle(double radius) { this(radius, "black"); }` — chains to 2-arg.
problems.hint3: `this(...)` must be the very FIRST statement inside a constructor body.
problems.editorial: Constructor chaining centralises initialization: all overloads ultimately call the most-specific constructor. this() MUST be the first statement — the compiler enforces this to ensure the object is always fully initialised through a single code path. Avoids duplicated field assignment logic.

problems.title: Inventory: Collection of Objects
problems.hint1: totalValue: `for (Product p : products) total += p.price * p.quantity;`
problems.hint2: findMostExpensive: initialise `Product max = products.get(0);` then iterate updating max.
problems.hint3: Or: `products.stream().max(Comparator.comparingDouble(p -> p.price)).orElse(null)`
problems.editorial: totalValue: 1.5×100 + 999×5 + 0.5×200 = 150 + 4995 + 100 = 5245.0. findMostExpensive tracks the Product with the highest unit price (not total value) — scan-and-update pattern with a running max.

problems.title: Mutable vs Immutable Point
problems.hint1: ImmutablePoint: `final int x, y;` — compiler prevents reassignment.
problems.hint2: `ImmutablePoint translate(int dx, int dy) { return new ImmutablePoint(x+dx, y+dy); }`
problems.hint3: Shared reference safety: pass the same ImmutablePoint to two methods — neither can alter the other's data.
problems.editorial: Immutability: final fields set once in the constructor, never changed. Every 'modification' returns a new object. The caller keeps the old reference untouched. This makes objects safe to share freely across threads, cache keys, and method parameters without defensive copying. String, Integer, LocalDate, BigDecimal all use this pattern.

problems.title: Temperature Converter Class
problems.description:
Design a `Temperature` class storing Celsius internally. Add factory methods `fromCelsius(double)`, `fromFahrenheit(double)`, `fromKelvin(double)`, and conversion getters `toCelsius()`, `toFahrenheit()`, `toKelvin()`.

**Example 1:**
Temperature.fromFahrenheit(212).toCelsius() => 100.0

**Example 2:**
Temperature.fromKelvin(273.15).toCelsius() => 0.0

**Example 3:**
Temperature.fromCelsius(0).toFahrenheit() => 32.0

**Key insight:** Factory methods communicate intent better than constructors — `fromFahrenheit(212)` is clearer than `new Temperature(100)`.
problems.hint1: Private constructor — only factory methods create instances: `private Temperature(double celsius) { this.celsius = celsius; }`
problems.hint2: fromFahrenheit: `return new Temperature((f - 32) * 5 / 9);`
problems.hint3: toFahrenheit: `return celsius * 9 / 5 + 32;` toKelvin: `return celsius + 273.15;`
problems.editorial: Static factory methods hide the constructor — the class controls how instances are created. Here all internal storage is Celsius; factory methods convert on entry, instance methods convert on output. This is how java.time.LocalDate.of(), Integer.valueOf(), and Optional.of() work — they control instance creation rather than exposing new directly.

problems.title: Compare Objects With Comparable
problems.hint1: Implement `public int compareTo(Student other)` in Student.
problems.hint2: Descending GPA: `return Double.compare(other.gpa, this.gpa);` (reversed).
problems.hint3: After `Collections.sort(list)`, iterate and print names to verify order.
problems.editorial: Comparable<T> defines the 'natural order' of objects. compareTo returns negative if this < other, 0 if equal, positive if this > other. For descending GPA: swap `this` and `other` in Double.compare. Collections.sort() and Arrays.sort() use this ordering. Alternatively use a Comparator for external ordering without modifying the class.
problems.sampleInput: [Charlie(3.5), Alice(3.9), Bob(3.7)] sorted by GPA desc

problems.title: Validate State in Constructor
problems.description:
Create `BankAccount(String owner, double initialBalance)`. The constructor must throw `IllegalArgumentException` if `owner` is blank or `initialBalance < 0`. `deposit(double)` and `withdraw(double)` must also validate inputs.

**Example 1:**
new BankAccount("Alice", 100.0).getBalance() => 100.0

**Example 2:**
new BankAccount("", 100.0) => throws IllegalArgumentException("Owner must not be blank")

**Example 3:**
new BankAccount("Bob", -50) => throws IllegalArgumentException("Balance cannot be negative")
problems.hint1: Check `if (owner == null || owner.isBlank()) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...);`
problems.hint2: Check `if (initialBalance < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...);`
problems.hint3: withdraw: also check `if (amount > balance) throw new IllegalStateException("Insufficient funds");`

testCases.output: caught: Owner must not be blank

problems.editorial: Constructor validation is the first line of defence — it ensures objects are always in a valid state from birth. If preconditions fail, throw IllegalArgumentException. If an operation would violate a business rule, throw IllegalStateException. This encapsulation principle means no external code can ever create a BankAccount with a negative balance.

problems.title: Object Graph — Order With Line Items
problems.hint1: Order has `List<LineItem> items = new ArrayList<>();`
problems.hint2: grandTotal: `items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.qty * i.unitPrice).sum()` or a loop.
problems.hint3: toString: iterate items with a StringBuilder, append each, add total at end.
problems.editorial: Composition: Order *contains* LineItems — has-a relationship. grandTotal: 2×12.99 + 10×1.50 + 1×49.99 = 25.98 + 15.00 + 49.99 = 90.97. This object graph pattern is the backbone of every e-commerce domain model.

problems.title: Singleton Pattern
problems.hint1: Private constructor prevents external instantiation.
problems.hint2: Private static inner class `Holder { static final DatabaseConfig INSTANCE = new DatabaseConfig(); }` — JVM initialises this lazily and thread-safely.
problems.hint3: `public static DatabaseConfig getInstance() { return Holder.INSTANCE; }`
problems.constraints: Single-class implementation. No synchronised keyword needed with holder pattern.
problems.editorial: Initialization-on-demand holder: the Holder inner class is not loaded until getInstance() is first called. Class loading in Java is inherently thread-safe — the JVM guarantees it happens exactly once. Compared to double-checked locking, this is simpler and provably correct. Used in Apache Commons, Spring framework internals.

problems.title: Defensive Copy in Constructor and Getter
problems.description:
Create an immutable `DateRange(Date start, Date end)` class. Since `java.util.Date` is mutable, the constructor must make **defensive copies**, and getters must return copies too — otherwise callers could mutate the "immutable" object.

**Example:**
Date s = new Date(1000L);
DateRange r = new DateRange(s, new Date(2000L));
s.setTime(9999L);          // mutate original
r.getStart().getTime() => 1000L  // defensive copy protects immutability

**Key insight:** `new Date(original.getTime())` in the constructor and getter prevents external mutation of internal state.
problems.hint1: In constructor: `this.start = new Date(start.getTime());` — copy, don't store the passed reference.
problems.hint2: In getStart(): `return new Date(start.getTime());` — return a copy, not the internal reference.
problems.hint3: Verify: get the start date, mutate it, then call getStart() again — it should return the original value.
problems.editorial: Effective Java Item 50: make defensive copies when fields are mutable objects. Two attack vectors: (1) caller mutates the object passed to the constructor — guard: copy in constructor. (2) caller mutates the object returned by a getter — guard: return a copy. Both must be defended. Modern Java avoids this with Instant or LocalDate which are already immutable.
problems.sampleInput: DateRange constructed, then source Date mutated

problems.title: Chained Validation Builder
problems.description:
Implement `UserBuilder` with fields `username`, `email`, `age`. `build()` must validate: username non-null/non-blank, email contains '@', age between 0–150. Throw `IllegalStateException` with a descriptive message on violation.

**Example 1:**
new UserBuilder().username("alice").email("alice@example.com").age(25).build()
=> User{username='alice', email='alice@example.com', age=25}

**Example 2:**
new UserBuilder().username("bob").email("not-an-email").age(25).build()
=> throws IllegalStateException("Invalid email")
problems.hint1: In build(): `List<String> errors = new ArrayList<>();` then add individual error messages.
problems.hint2: After all checks: `if (!errors.isEmpty()) throw new IllegalStateException(String.join("; ", errors));`
problems.hint3: Email check: `if (!email.contains("@")) errors.add("email must contain @");`

testCases.output: username must be 3-20 chars; email must contain @; age must be 18-120

problems.constraints: All three fields must be set.
problems.editorial: Collect-then-throw validation: all rules run and errors accumulate into a List. Only at the end, if any errors exist, join them and throw a single exception. This is far better UX than fail-fast (one error per attempt) — the user sees all problems at once. Used in Bean Validation (Hibernate Validator), Spring's BindingResult, and GraphQL error handling.

problems.title: Generic Pair Class
problems.description:
Implement a generic `Pair<A, B>` class with fields `first` and `second`, a full constructor, getters, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`, and a static factory method `of(A a, B b)`.

**Example 1:**
Pair<String, Integer> p = Pair.of("hello", 42);
p.getFirst() => "hello"
p.getSecond() => 42
p.toString() => "(hello, 42)"

**Example 2:**
Pair.of("a", 1).equals(Pair.of("a", 1)) => true

**Key insight:** Generic classes let you write type-safe containers without casting. `Pair<String,Integer>` is checked at compile time.
problems.hint1: Class declaration: `public class Pair<A, B>` with fields `private final A first; private final B second;`
problems.hint2: `swap()` returns `new Pair<>(second, first)` — type arguments infer automatically.
problems.hint3: equals: check instanceof Pair<?,?>, cast, then `Objects.equals(first, other.first) && Objects.equals(second, other.second)`
problems.constraints: A, B can be any non-primitive type.
problems.editorial: Generic class: type parameters A and B replace concrete types, making Pair reusable. swap() returns Pair<B,A> — generics track the type swap. equals uses instanceof Pair<?,?> (wildcard) since generics are erased at runtime. Objects.equals handles nulls safely. Map.Entry<K,V> in the JDK follows this exact pattern.

problems.title: Chained Object Transformations (Pipeline)
problems.description:
Create a `StringPipeline` class wrapping a String with chainable transformation methods: `trim()`, `toUpperCase()`, `replace(from, to)`, `append(s)`. Each returns `this` for chaining. `result()` returns the final string.

**Example:**
new StringPipeline("  hello world  ")
    .trim()
    .toUpperCase()
    .replace("WORLD", "JAVA")
    .append("!")
    .result()
=> "HELLO JAVA!"

**Key insight:** Each method mutates internal state and returns `this` — the pipeline pattern for readable sequential transformations.
problems.hint1: Each method: `return new StringPipeline(this.value.trim());` — creates new wrapper.
problems.hint2: This is different from mutable chaining (return this) — each step is a new object.
problems.hint3: `get()` simply returns the wrapped String value.
problems.editorial: Immutable chaining: each step creates a new StringPipeline rather than modifying the existing one. The original StringPipeline is never changed — safe to branch: `StringPipeline base = new StringPipeline("hi"); StringPipeline a = base.append("!"); StringPipeline b = base.append("?")`  — base is unaffected. This mirrors Java's Stream API and Optional — each operation returns a new wrapper.
problems.sampleInput: "  hello world  " -> trim -> toUpperCase -> replace(WORLD,JAVA) -> append(!)

problems.title: equals() Without hashCode() — Bug Demo
problems.hint1: Without hashCode: k1 and k2 hash to different buckets (identity hash). HashMap looks in wrong bucket.
problems.hint2: With Objects.hash(id) as hashCode: both hash to same bucket, equals confirms match.
problems.hint3: Return a map of results: `brokenGet` = null, `fixedGet` = some value.
problems.constraints: No constraint — this is a deliberate bug demonstration.
problems.editorial: HashMap uses hashCode first to find the bucket. If hashCode() is not overridden, two logically-equal objects (same id, both returning true from equals) hash to different buckets — the entry is never found. The contract: if a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). Override both or neither. IDEs and code quality tools (SpotBugs, SonarQube) flag this violation.
problems.sampleInput: put Key(1)->"first", put Key(1)->"second" (no hashCode override)

problems.title: Value Object Pattern — Money
problems.description:
Implement an immutable `Money(long cents, String currency)` value object. Override `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`, and add `add(Money other)` (must have same currency) and `multiply(int factor)`.

**Example 1:**
new Money(1000, "USD").toString() => "$10.00 USD"

**Example 2:**
new Money(500, "USD").add(new Money(300, "USD")) => Money(800, "USD")

**Example 3:**
new Money(200, "USD").add(new Money(100, "EUR")) => throws IllegalArgumentException("Currency mismatch")
problems.hint1: Store cents as long to avoid floating-point errors. $1.99 = 199 cents.
problems.hint2: add(): if (!currency.equals(other.currency)) throw new IllegalArgumentException(). Return new Money(cents + other.cents, currency).
problems.hint3: toString(): format cents to dollars: `String.format("%s %.2f", currency, cents / 100.0)`
problems.editorial: Value Object: identity comes from value, not reference. Two Money(500,USD) objects are equal — just like two Integer(5) objects are equal. Immutability + equals/hashCode makes Money safe as a HashMap key and for arithmetic. Storing money as cents (long) avoids IEEE 754 floating-point errors — $0.1 + $0.2 = $0.30000000000000004 in doubles but 10+20=30 cents exactly.

problems.title: Prototype Pattern — Clone Objects
problems.hint1: Define abstract `Shape` with `abstract Shape clone();` and common fields like `color`.
problems.hint2: Circle.clone(): `return new Circle(this.radius, this.color);`
problems.hint3: ShapeRegistry uses `Map<String, Shape> prototypes`. `create(name)` calls `prototypes.get(name).clone()`.
problems.constraints: Registry must be pre-populated before create().
problems.editorial: Prototype pattern: instead of calling new SpecificClass() everywhere, clone a pre-built prototype. Useful when object construction is expensive or when you want to produce objects that are pre-configured. Each clone() creates a fully independent deep copy — mutating c1 does NOT affect the stored prototype. Used in Java's object serialisation round-trip cloning and game entity factories.
